content.text: Immutable Build Artifacts for Claude Services
An immutable build artifact is one that
cannot change after it is created

. For Claude services, this means the container image, the prompt files baked into it, and the model version declared in configuration are fixed at build time. Any change — including a one-line prompt edit — requires a new build that re-runs every CI gate.
Mutability is the enemy of reproducibility. If the artifact that passed CI is not byte-for-byte identical to what runs in production, your CI gates are testing a fiction.

This sub-subtopic covers how to construct a genuinely immutable artifact for a Claude service, what belongs inside vs. outside the image, how to pin all dependencies (including the base image), and how to verify immutability at deploy time.

attrs.alt: Immutable build artifact anatomy: container image layers and deployment manifest

content.text: What 'Immutable' Means in Practice
Immutability has four properties, all of which must hold simultaneously:
Property
What it means
How to enforce
Content-addressed identity
The artifact is referenced by its SHA-256 digest, not a mutable tag
Use image@sha256:… in deployments, never :latest
No post-build modification
The image layers cannot be altered after the build job exits
Use a registry with immutable tags enabled (ECR, Artifact Registry)
Dependency pinning
Every dependency — including the OS base image — is pinned to an exact digest or hash
requirements.txt with hashes; FROM with digest
Prompt-as-code
Prompt files are baked into the image at build time, not mounted or fetched at runtime
COPY prompts/ /app/prompts/ in Dockerfile
Dockerfile for a Claude Service

A correctly structured Dockerfile for a Claude service pins the base image by digest, copies prompt files as a separate layer (so prompt-only changes invalidate only that layer), and excludes secrets entirely.

# Pin base image by digest — mutable tags like python:3.12-slim can change
FROM python:3.12-slim@sha256:e56acc77e35eb4d8e4cb85c2dc41c7f6bf00e50a5a45f1a79d5ecdda23b7a73

WORKDIR /app

# Dependency layer: copy lockfile first for layer caching
COPY requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Prompt layer: separate so prompt changes don't invalidate the app layer
COPY prompts/ ./prompts/

# Application code layer
COPY src/ ./src/

# Record build metadata at image build time
ARG BUILD_SHA
ARG PROMPT_HASH
ARG MODEL_VERSION
ENV BUILD_SHA=${BUILD_SHA}
ENV PROMPT_HASH=${PROMPT_HASH}
ENV MODEL_VERSION=${MODEL_VERSION}

# Runtime secrets injected via environment — NEVER baked in
# ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set by the orchestration layer at deploy time

CMD ["python", "-m", "src.app"]
Why Prompts Are Baked In

Fetching prompts from a config store or database at runtime introduces a mutable surface: the deployed container could serve different behaviour depending on when it starts. Baking prompts into the image means the CI-tested behaviour is exactly what runs in production — no runtime divergence.
If a prompt needs to change, a new image is built and promoted through all gates
Environment-specific prompt variants (staging uses a slightly softer tone) are handled by building separate images with a BUILD_ARG, not by runtime injection
Emergency prompt changes are still a full build + gate pass — the gates are fast enough (minutes) that this is acceptable for all non-critical issues
Pinning Python Dependencies

A requirements.txt without hashes is mutable: PyPI can serve a different version of a package after the lock was written. Use pip-compile with hash verification to produce a fully reproducible dependency set:

# Generate a hash-locked requirements.txt from requirements.in
pip-compile   --generate-hashes   --output-file requirements.txt   requirements.in

# Install with hash verification (fails if any package hash mismatches)
pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt

# requirements.txt (excerpt — hash-locked)
anthropic==0.40.0     --hash=sha256:1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b     --hash=sha256:9f8e7d6c5b4a3928170f6e5d4c3b2a19f8e7d6c5b4a392817...
Content-Addressed Image References

Docker tags like :latest or :v1.2.3 are mutable pointers. The same tag can point to a different image after a push. Always deploy by digest to guarantee what runs in production matches what was tested:

# Build and capture the digest
docker build   --build-arg BUILD_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)   --build-arg PROMPT_HASH=$(python3 -c "from prompts import prompt_hash; print(prompt_hash('customer_support'))")   --build-arg MODEL_VERSION=claude-haiku-4-5-20251001   -t myregistry.io/claude-app:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)   .

# Push and capture the digest
DIGEST=$(docker push myregistry.io/claude-app:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)   | grep "digest:" | awk "{print \$3}")

echo "IMAGE_DIGEST=$DIGEST" >> $GITHUB_ENV   # expose to next steps

# .github/workflows/build.yml (simplified build step)
      - name: Build and push image
        id: build
        run: |
          TAG=myregistry.io/claude-app:${{ github.sha }}
          docker build             --build-arg BUILD_SHA=${{ github.sha }}             --build-arg PROMPT_HASH=$(python3 -c "from prompts import prompt_hash; print(prompt_hash('customer_support'))")             --build-arg MODEL_VERSION=claude-haiku-4-5-20251001             -t $TAG .
          DIGEST=$(docker push $TAG | grep "digest:" | awk "{print \$3}")
          echo "image_digest=$DIGEST" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Generate manifest
        run: |
          python3 scripts/generate_manifest.py             --image-digest ${{ steps.build.outputs.image_digest }}             --build-sha ${{ github.sha }}             --eval-pass-rate ${{ steps.eval.outputs.pass_rate }}             --probes-passed ${{ steps.security.outputs.probes_passed }}             --probes-total ${{ steps.security.outputs.probes_total }}
Verifying Immutability at Deploy Time
Before deploying, verify that the manifest's recorded image digest matches what is in the registry. This catches accidental tag re-use or registry mis-configuration:

# scripts/verify_artifact.py
"""Verify that the manifest digest matches the registry before deploying."""
import json, subprocess, sys, pathlib

def get_registry_digest(image_ref: str) -> str:
    """Return the actual digest of an image in the registry."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["docker", "manifest", "inspect", "--verbose", image_ref],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    import json as _json
    data = _json.loads(result.stdout)
    if isinstance(data, list):
        data = data[0]
    return data.get("Descriptor", {}).get("digest", "")

def verify_manifest(manifest_path: str, image_ref: str):
    manifest = json.loads(pathlib.Path(manifest_path).read_text())
    expected_digest = manifest["image_digest"]
    actual_digest = get_registry_digest(image_ref)

    if actual_digest != expected_digest:
        print(f"ARTIFACT MISMATCH")
        print(f"  Expected: {expected_digest}")
        print(f"  Actual:   {actual_digest}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f"Artifact verified: {actual_digest[:30]}...")
    print(f"  prompt_hash: {manifest['prompt_hash']}")
    print(f"  eval_pass_rate: {manifest['eval_pass_rate']:.1%}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    verify_manifest(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
Registry Configuration
The registry itself must enforce immutability. Both major cloud registries support this:
Registry
Immutable tag setting
Effect
AWS ECR
Tag immutability: IMMUTABLE
PutImage fails if the tag already exists — forces new tag per push
GCP Artifact Registry
Immutable tags policy
Re-tagging an existing tag is rejected
GitHub Container Registry
Package settings → immutable
Digest-based references always return the original content
Even with immutable tags enabled, always reference images by digest in deployment configurations. Tags can still be moved by an admin; digests cannot.
Reproducible Builds

A fully immutable artifact is not just immutable after creation — it is also reproducible. Given the same inputs (source code commit, requirements.txt, prompt files), the build should produce the same image. This requires eliminating non-determinism in the build:
No pip install without a lockfile:
Always use
--require-hashes
No apt-get install without version pins:
apt-get install -y curl=7.88.1-10+deb12u5
not
apt-get install -y curl
No COPY . . as the first layer:
Copy dependency files first so code changes don't invalidate the dependency layer.
SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH:
Set
SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=0
in the build environment to remove filesystem timestamp non-determinism from the image layer hashes.

# Reproducibility improvements
FROM python:3.12-slim@sha256:<pinned-digest>

# Set SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH to eliminate timestamp non-determinism
ARG SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=0
ENV SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH=${SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH}

WORKDIR /app

# Pin apt packages with version
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends     curl=7.88.1-10+deb12u5   && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --require-hashes -r requirements.txt

COPY prompts/ ./prompts/
COPY src/ ./src/

ARG BUILD_SHA
ENV BUILD_SHA=${BUILD_SHA}
CMD ["python", "-m", "src.app"]
Model Version Pinning

The model version is part of the immutable artifact contract. If the model version is read from an environment variable at runtime, different invocations of the same image can use different models — defeating the purpose of immutability.

# src/app.py — model version baked in at build time, not read from env
import os

# Build-time constant: injected as ENV in Dockerfile from --build-arg MODEL_VERSION
# This means every instance of this image uses exactly the same model.
MODEL_VERSION = os.environ["MODEL_VERSION"]  # fails fast if build arg missing

from anthropic import Anthropic
client = Anthropic()

def chat(system: str, user_message: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=MODEL_VERSION,   # fixed per image build
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

When Anthropic releases a new model version, the upgrade is a deliberate build: update the MODEL_VERSION build arg, rebuild, re-run all gates, and promote through environments. This ensures the eval suite and security probes have validated behaviour on the new model before it serves production traffic.
Handling Snapshot vs. Stable Model References
Reference style
Example
Suitable for
Risk
Date-stamped
claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
Production images
None — frozen to a specific snapshot
Minor version alias
claude-haiku-4-5
Nightly eval runs only
Model can change under alias; never in production
Latest alias
claude-haiku
Never
Any model update changes production behaviour silently
Artifact Retention and Garbage Collection
Immutable artifacts accumulate. A build every 30 minutes produces 1,440 images per month. Retention policy balances auditability against storage cost:

# scripts/artifact_retention.py
"""Apply retention policy: keep the last N builds and all production-deployed images."""
import json, pathlib, subprocess, datetime
from typing import Set

MANIFEST_DIR = pathlib.Path("manifests/")
KEEP_RECENT = 30          # always keep the 30 most recent builds
KEEP_DEPLOYED: Set[str]  # digests of currently-deployed images (from cluster)

def get_deployed_digests(namespace: str = "prod") -> Set[str]:
    """Query the cluster for all image digests currently running."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "get", "pods", "-n", namespace,
         "-o", "jsonpath={.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    images = result.stdout.split()
    # Filter to digest references (sha256:...)
    return {img for img in images if "@sha256:" in img}

def candidates_for_deletion(manifests: list[dict], deployed: Set[str]) -> list[dict]:
    """Return manifests that are neither in the recent window nor deployed."""
    # Sort by build timestamp descending
    sorted_m = sorted(manifests, key=lambda m: m["build_timestamp"], reverse=True)
    recent = {m["image_digest"] for m in sorted_m[:KEEP_RECENT]}
    delete = []
    for m in sorted_m[KEEP_RECENT:]:
        if m["image_digest"] not in deployed and m["image_digest"] not in recent:
            delete.append(m)
    return delete

def load_manifests() -> list[dict]:
    return [json.loads(p.read_text()) for p in MANIFEST_DIR.glob("*.json")]
Always cross-reference with the cluster before deleting: deleting an image that is still running in a stopped pod (kept for forensics) breaks rollback.
Build Metadata Labels

Bake searchable metadata into the image using OCI standard labels. This makes it possible to find the source commit, prompt version, and CI run for any running container without consulting an external database:

# Standard OCI image labels — queryable via docker inspect
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.revision="${BUILD_SHA}"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.created="${BUILD_TIMESTAMP}"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.source="https://github.com/acme/claude-app"
LABEL org.opencontainers.image.version="${IMAGE_VERSION}"

# Claude-service-specific labels
LABEL ai.claude.prompt_hash="${PROMPT_HASH}"
LABEL ai.claude.model="${MODEL_VERSION}"
LABEL ai.claude.eval_pass_rate="${EVAL_PASS_RATE}"

# Query labels from any running container
docker inspect myregistry.io/claude-app@sha256:abc123   --format "{{json .Config.Labels}}" | jq .

# Find which build SHA is running in a Kubernetes pod
kubectl get pod claude-app-xyz -o jsonpath="{.spec.containers[0].image}"   | xargs docker inspect --format "{{index .Config.Labels "org.opencontainers.image.revision"}}"
Common Pitfalls
Using :latest in the Dockerfile FROM:
The base image changes silently on the next build. Always use digest.
Fetching prompts at startup from a config store:
The running container may serve different prompts than what CI tested. Bake prompts in.
Injecting MODEL_VERSION as a runtime env var:
A model version change is a semantic change that must go through CI gates. Bake it in as a build arg.
Rebuilding for each environment:
A staging rebuild is not the same artifact that passed CI. Build once, promote the digest.
Not storing manifests:
Without retained manifests, rollback requires guessing which previous image digest was the last good build.
Summary

An immutable artifact has four properties: content-addressed identity (digest reference), no post-build modification (registry immutability), dependency pinning (hash-locked requirements), and prompt-as-code (prompts baked in, not fetched at runtime)
Pin the base image by its SHA-256 digest in the FROM instruction; mutable tags like :slim can point to a different image after a push
Use pip-compile --generate-hashes to produce a hash-verified requirements.txt; install with --require-hashes to fail on any mismatch
Always deploy using the image digest, not a mutable tag; capture the digest from docker push output and record it in the deployment manifest
Enable immutable tag policies in your container registry (ECR, Artifact Registry) to prevent accidental tag overwrite
Verify the manifest digest against the registry before deploying; a mismatch means the artifact in the manifest is not what is in the registry
Eliminate build non-determinism (SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH, pinned apt packages, ordered COPY instructions) to make builds reproducible from the same source inputs
